html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 事有难言聊志怪 - Means 'when things are difficult to express, one writes about strange occurrences.'
  • 人非吾与更搜神 - Means 'if one is not a confidant, one searches for the divine.'
  • 子虚乌有 - Phrase meaning 'fictitious' or 'imaginary.'
  • 不语怪 - Confucian principle of not discussing strange or supernatural matters.
  • 醉茶子 - Pseudonym or nickname, possibly referring to the author or a character.
  • 落拓一衿 - Describes a person who is carefree and unbound by convention.
  • 寒窗坐老 - Means 'to spend one's life in study and solitude.'
  • 抚时感事 - Means 'to reflect on the times and events.'
  • 篇什 - Refers to poems or literary works.
  • 当头棒喝 - Buddhist term meaning 'a sudden, forceful awakening.'
  • 光绪壬辰仲冬庸叟杨光仪识 - Colophon indicating the date and author's name: '光绪壬辰' (1892), '仲冬' (mid-winter), '庸叟杨光仪' (Yang Guangyi).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蒲留仙志异 - Refers to *Liaozhai Zhiyi* (Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio) by Pu Songling.
  • 文达公五种 - Refers to the five works compiled by Wén Zhenheng of the Ming dynasty.
  • 光绪壬辰 - The year 1892 during the Guangxu era of the Qing dynasty.
  • 志怪 - Genre of literature recording strange or supernatural events.

Proper Nouns

  • 蒲留仙 - Pu Songling, author of *Liaozhai Zhiyi*.
  • 文达公 - Wén Zhenheng, scholar and writer of the Ming dynasty.
  • 光绪壬辰 - The year 1892 during the Guangxu era of the Qing dynasty.
  • 庸叟杨光仪 - The author of the colophon, Yang Guangyi.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 自叙 - Title indicating the author's own introduction or preface.
  • 留仙叟 - Respectful term for a scholar or writer, likely referring to Pu Songling.
  • 五种传奇 - Refers to five types of legendary stories, possibly including *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*.
  • 文达公 - Respectful term for a literary figure, likely referring to Pu Songling.
  • 言能警世 - Means 'words that can warn the world,' emphasizing the moral and social impact of the writing.
  • 创之匪易 - Means 'creating it is not easy,' highlighting the difficulty of original creation.
  • 捷足者既已先登 - Means 'those who are quick-footed have already climbed first,' referring to those who have succeeded in creating something new.
  • 继之殊难 - Means 'following it is particularly difficult,' emphasizing the challenge of continuing a tradition.
  • 后来者莫能居上 - Means 'later comers cannot surpass them,' indicating that it is hard to outdo the original creators.
  • 言念及此，兴致索然 - Means 'thinking of this, interest wanes,' expressing the author's loss of enthusiasm.
  • 人各有怀，甘苦不同共际遇 - Means 'people have different feelings, joys, and sorrows in their encounters,' reflecting the diversity of human experiences.
  • 士非得志，穷愁每见于词章 - Means 'scholars who do not achieve their goals often express their poverty and sorrow in their writings,' highlighting the emotional depth of literary works.
  • 惟文字厌弃夫平庸 - Means 'only words reject the ordinary,' emphasizing the desire for excellence in writing.
  • 搜讨乐言夫鬼怪 - Means 'enjoy discussing ghosts and monsters,' indicating the author's interest in supernatural topics.
  • 性有偏好，口讵能缄 - Means 'having preferences, how can one keep silent,' expressing the author's inclination to share his interests.
  • 文不求工，狂且弗顾 - Means 'not seeking perfection in writing, and not caring about being wild,' indicating a casual and unrestrained approach to writing.
  • 辟诸自鸣其天籁 - Means 'like a bird singing its own natural voice,' emphasizing the authenticity and spontaneity of the writing.
  • 奚避雷同 - Means 'why avoid sameness,' suggesting that some repetition or similarity is acceptable.
  • 博雅君子所指摘者矣 - Means 'it will be criticized by learned gentlemen,' acknowledging potential criticism from knowledgeable readers.
  • 传记降神，易占载鬼 - Refers to the inclusion of supernatural elements in literature and divination.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 留仙叟 - Likely refers to Pu Songling, a famous Chinese writer known for his *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*.
  • 五种传奇 - Refers to five types of legendary stories, possibly including *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*.
  • 文达公 - Likely refers to Pu Songling, emphasizing his literary achievements.
  • 言能警世 - Emphasizes the moral and social impact of the writing, reflecting the Confucian tradition of literature as a means of moral instruction.
  • 传记降神，易占载鬼 - Refers to the inclusion of supernatural elements in literature and divination, reflecting the rich tradition of Chinese mythology and folklore.

Proper Nouns

  • 留仙叟 - Respectful term for a scholar or writer, likely referring to Pu Songling.
  • 五种传奇 - Refers to five types of legendary stories, possibly including *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*.
  • 文达公 - Respectful term for a literary figure, likely referring to Pu Songling.
  • 桑号子明 - A story about a mulberry tree calling out to a child named Ming.
  • 龟呼元绪 - A story about a turtle calling out to a man named Yuanxu.
  • 螺壳或藏彼美 - A story about a snail shell containing a beautiful woman.
  • 鹅笼或寄书生 - A story about a goose cage containing a scholar.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 仆半生抑郁，累日长愁 - Classical expression meaning 'I have been depressed for half my life, enduring long days of sorrow.'
  • 扫愁帚 - Metaphorical term meaning 'a broom to sweep away sorrow.'
  • 闻则记之，聊以自娱 - Means 'I would write down what I heard, just to amuse myself.'
  • 高山流水 - Classical allusion to the friendship between Yu Boya and Zhong Ziqi, symbolizing deep friendship and shared understanding.
  • 弄月吟风 - Metaphor for engaging in poetic or literary pursuits.
  • 牢骚之客 - Refers to those who are prone to complaining or expressing dissatisfaction.
  • 东坡说鬼 - Refers to Su Shi (Su Dongpo), a famous Song Dynasty poet known for his ghost stories.
  • 干宝搜神 - Refers to Gan Bao, a Western Jin Dynasty author known for his work *In Search of the Supernatural* (*搜神记*).
  • 兰因絮果 - Metaphor for a beautiful beginning leading to an unfortunate end, often used to describe romantic relationships.
  • 贞木贪泉 - Metaphor for maintaining integrity even in corrupt environments.
  • 微加月旦 - Metaphor for subtle criticism or evaluation.
  • 事或关乎报应，词不背乎圣贤也 - Means 'the matters may relate to retribution, and the words do not contradict the sages.'
  • 知我昔其谅之哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Can anyone understand and forgive me in the past?'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 中书君 - Term for the imperial chancellor or a high-ranking official.
  • 二三良朋 - Refers to a few good friends.
  • 蜗舍 - A humble abode, often used to describe a modest home.
  • 华表 - A traditional Chinese stone pillar often found in front of palaces or temples.
  • 狐烹 - Reference to foxes being cooked, which can be a metaphor for supernatural occurrences.
  • 三尺荒坟 - Refers to a small, neglected grave.
  • 鬼唱 - Describes the sound of ghosts singing, often associated with eerie or supernatural events.
  • 信以传信，清谈增鬼火之光 - Refers to the idea that true stories enhance the light of ghostly fires during discussions.
  • 玄之又玄 - Phrase from the *Tao Te Ching*, meaning 'mysterious and profound.'
  • 雅谑生幽魂之色 - Describes how elegant jokes bring out the color of ghostly spirits.
  • 光绪壬辰冬日 - Refers to the winter of the year 1892 during the Guangxu reign of the Qing Dynasty.

Proper Nouns

  • 仆 - The narrator, using the first-person pronoun 'I.'
  • 东坡 - Su Shi (Su Dongpo), a famous Song Dynasty poet.
  • 干宝 - Gan Bao, a Western Jin Dynasty author.
  • 兰因絮果 - Metaphor for a beautiful beginning leading to an unfortunate end.
  • 贞木贪泉 - Metaphor for maintaining integrity in corrupt environments.
  • 醉茶子 - A pseudonym or pen name used by the author.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 折獄 - Literal meaning is 'breaking a case,' but it refers to solving a legal case or dispute.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 青手印 - Literal phrase possibly referring to a 'green-handprint' or 'blue handprint,' denoting a supernatural mark or magical symbol. The color '青' (qīng) can mean green, blue, or dark, and may carry symbolic meaning.
  • 天官 - Term from Chinese cosmology referring to the celestial bureaucracy or officials of Heaven. It may also refer to a specific deity or rank in the Taoist pantheon.
  • 返魂 - Literally 'returning the soul,' associated with resurrection, reincarnation, or soul-retrieval in Chinese folklore.
  • 鬼市 - A 'ghost market,' referring to a place where supernatural beings or objects are traded, often at night.
  • 投胎 - A well-known term meaning 'reincarnation' or 'rebirth in a new body.'
  • 冥報 - 'Report to the Underworld' or 'judgment in the afterlife,' referring to karmic retribution in the netherworld.
  • 鬼馔 - 'Ghost food,' possibly referring to food prepared for spirits or food that is consumed by ghosts.
  • 宅仙 - 'Household deity' or 'resident spirit' of a home, often a protective or mischievous entity.
  • 旱魃 - A mythical creature or demon responsible for causing droughts.
  • 夙債 - 'Old debt' or 'karmic debt,' often used in the context of past-life obligations or karma.
  • 疑案 - 'Mysterious case' or 'unsolved mystery,' often found in ghost stories or judicial tales.
  • 樹妖 - 'Tree demon' or 'tree spirit,' a common figure in Chinese folklore.
  • 狐仙 - 'Fox spirit,' a well-known supernatural being in Chinese mythology, often associated with transformation and seduction.
  • 鼠技 - 'Mouse skill' or 'rat tricks,' possibly referring to mischievous or deceptive actions, often attributed to trickster spirits.
  • 蛇卵 - 'Snake egg,' possibly a metaphor or literal reference to a magical or dangerous object.
  • 金目怪 - 'Golden-eyed monster,' a term for a supernatural being with golden eyes, often associated with danger or divinity.
  • 雷報 - 'Thunder retribution,' a term used to describe divine punishment through thunder or lightning.
  • 魅戲 - 'Spirit’s play' or 'demon’s performance,' possibly referring to a supernatural illusion or trick.
  • 怪風 - 'Strange wind,' often used to describe unnatural or supernatural weather events.
  • 冷香堂 - 'Cold Scent Hall,' possibly the name of a place or a supernatural entity with a cold, fragrant presence.
  • 疫鬼 - 'Plague spirit' or 'disease demon,' a supernatural being associated with illness or epidemics.
  • 狐妻 - 'Fox wife,' a common trope in Chinese folklore where a fox spirit takes on a human form and marries a human.
  • 棺怪 - 'Coffin monster,' likely referring to a supernatural entity that resides in or emerges from a coffin.
  • 畫妖 - 'Painted demon' or 'picture spirit,' a being that comes to life from a painting.
  • 小黃人 - 'Little yellow people,' a term that may be used to describe small, yellow-skinned beings, possibly from folklore or fantasy.
  • 金雞 - 'Golden rooster,' possibly a symbolic or magical bird, or a deity associated with the rooster.
  • 金龜 - 'Golden tortoise,' a term that may refer to a mythical creature or a symbolic object.
  • 鬼哭 - 'Ghost cry,' a term used to describe eerie, mournful sounds attributed to the supernatural.
  • 樹哭 - 'Tree cry,' possibly a metaphor for the suffering or death of a tree, or a supernatural phenomenon.
  • 狐崇 - 'Fox reverence' or 'fox worship,' possibly a term for a cult or belief system centered around fox spirits.
  • 蛛怪 - 'Spider monster,' a supernatural creature in the form of a spider.
  • 二豎 - 'Two little ones,' possibly referring to two small spirits or demons.
  • 木妖 - 'Wood demon' or 'tree spirit,' a supernatural being associated with trees or wood.
  • 丐人子 - 'Beggar’s child,' a term for a child from a beggar family, possibly used in a story context.
  • 衣怪 - 'Clothing demon,' a supernatural being associated with clothing or garments.

Proper Nouns

  • 張孝子 - Proper noun referring to a historical or legendary figure known for filial piety.
  • 劉暉 - Likely a historical or fictional person.
  • 吳某 - Generic term for a person surnamed Wu, possibly a character in a story.
  • 毛某 - Generic term for a person surnamed Mao.
  • 申某 - Generic term for a person surnamed Shen.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卷三 - A chapter or section heading, likely from a larger compilation of stories or tales, indicating this is the third volume or part.
  • 化犬 - A phrase meaning 'transformed into a dog,' often used in Chinese folklore to describe a person who has been cursed or transformed into an animal.
  • 鬼吟詩 - 'Ghost reciting poetry,' referring to a ghost that utters poetic verses, a common motif in Chinese ghost stories.
  • 冥報 - 'Retribution in the afterlife' or 'karmic retribution in the underworld,' a concept from Chinese Buddhist and Daoist beliefs.
  • 夢詩 - 'Dream poetry,' a poetic composition or verse that appears in a dream, often with symbolic or prophetic meaning.
  • 乩仙 - 'Spirit medium' or 'spirit tablet oracle,' referring to a method of divination using a spirit tablet (乩) to receive messages from the supernatural.
  • 矢魔 - Possibly a compound word meaning 'arrow demon' or 'arrow spirit,' though the term is not commonly found in classical texts and may be a variant or dialectical form.
  • 倭某 - 'A certain Japanese person,' referring to a Japanese individual, as '倭' (Wō) was historically used to refer to people from Japan.
  • 擒風 - Literally 'capture the wind,' but likely a metaphor or idiom, possibly meaning to control or capture something intangible or elusive.
  • 古瓦罐 - 'Ancient clay jar,' a common item in Chinese folklore, often associated with hidden treasures or magical properties.
  • 鐵叉 - 'Iron fork,' possibly a weapon or tool used in a story.
  • 磁鶴 - 'Magnetic crane' or 'magnet crane,' likely a magical or supernatural object.
  • 產異 - 'Strange birth' or 'birth anomaly,' referring to unusual or miraculous births.
  • 古磁器 - 'Ancient magnetic pottery,' possibly referring to ancient ceramic items with unusual properties.
  • 徽商 - 'Huizhou merchants,' a historically prominent merchant group from the Huizhou region in Anhui province.
  • 狐帽 - 'Fox hat,' possibly a metaphor or a reference to a fox spirit or a hat with magical properties.
  • 鼠媪 - 'Mouse woman' or 'mouse witch,' a female supernatural being in the form of a mouse or associated with mice.
  • 稻田鬼 - 'Rice field ghost,' a ghost associated with rice fields, possibly a spirit of a farmer or a cursed being.
  • 疟鬼 - 'Malaria ghost' or 'fever demon,' referring to a supernatural cause of illness, particularly malaria.
  • 山左布 - 'Mountain left cloth' or 'left side of the mountain cloth,' possibly a term for a type of fabric or a reference to a location.

Proper Nouns

  • 張公 - A title or name of a character, possibly a scholar or official.
  • 娄某 - A character with the surname Liu, but the name is unspecified.
  • 冯君三异 - 'Feng Jun’s Three Oddities,' possibly a story involving three strange events or characters.
  • 白塔寺 - A temple in China, possibly a real or fictional location.
  • 李志青 - A character name.
  • 陶生 - A scholar or student named Tao.
  • 定兴城隍 - The city god of Dingxing, a location in Hebei province.
  • 冤妇 - A common theme in Chinese literature, referring to a woman wrongfully accused or mistreated.
  • 鼠怪 - A supernatural being in the form of a mouse.
  • 黑山大王 - A supernatural entity or leader of a ghostly force.
  • 龙眠穴 - A cave associated with dragons or magical sleep.
  • 冯氏仆 - A servant from the Feng family.
  • 於菟大鬼 - 'Yu Tu Great Ghost,' 'Yu Tu' is an ancient term for tiger, so this may be a 'Great Tiger Ghost.'
  • 李茂才 - A character name.
  • 花果楼 - A location or building.
  • 龚姓 - A family or person with the surname Gong.
  • 陈氏怪 - A strange or supernatural event involving the Chen family.
  • 娇娥 - A name or title for a beautiful young woman.
  • 岳某 - A character with the surname Yue.
  • 古剑 - A magical or historical sword.
  • 茔中怪 - A supernatural being in a burial ground.
  • 山神 - A deity of the mountains.
  • 铜骡 - A magical or supernatural animal.
  • 蛤佛 - Possibly a miswriting of '蛤蟆佛' (Toad Buddha), a deity or spirit associated with toads.
  • 泥娃 - A child made of clay, possibly a magical or cursed being.
  • 产叟 - An elderly woman involved in childbirth, possibly with supernatural elements.
  • 张氏妇 - A woman from the Zhang family.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 冤魂 - Wronged spirit or victimized soul, referring to the spirits of the deceased who died unjustly or under tragic circumstances.
  • 大人迹 - Trace of a great person, likely referring to a legend or footprint of a notable figure, possibly a deity or historical personage.
  • 二仙 - Two immortals or two sages, a common term in Chinese folklore for supernatural beings.
  • 槐仙 - Willow-ghost or willow spirit, possibly a specific type of spirit or deity associated with willow trees.
  • 张千总 - Name or title, possibly of a historical or legendary figure; '张' is a common surname, '千总' may be a military or official rank.
  • 王金铎 - Proper name, likely of a historical or legendary figure.
  • 邵明 - Proper name, possibly a person or a title.
  • 鬼窃饮 - Ghost steals to drink, referring to a supernatural act of theft or consumption.
  • 粤东童 - Child from the East of Guangdong, referring to someone from the southern Chinese region of Guangdong.
  • 营弁 - Military officer or rank, likely from the Qing or earlier dynasties.
  • 夙冤 - Ancient grudge or long-standing feud, often used in the context of karmic or supernatural revenge.
  • 孙某 - Generic name, possibly a placeholder for a person.
  • 黄鼠 - Yellow rat or field mouse, often used symbolically or in fables.
  • 粥厂灾异 - Disaster at the porridge factory, possibly referring to an event involving a charitable institution or disaster in a place where porridge is distributed.
  • 林承嗣 - Proper name, likely of a historical or legendary figure.
  • 猪龙 - Pig dragon, a term that may refer to a mythical creature or a mischievous spirit.
  • 二鼠 - Two rats, possibly symbolic or referring to a specific pair of rats in a story.
  • 蝶仙 - Butterfly immortal or butterfly spirit, a common motif in Chinese folklore.
  • 冥狱 - Underworld prison or hell, the place of punishment for the dead.
  • 观花爆 - Watching flower fireworks, likely a festival or ritual involving fireworks and flowers.
  • 蜂异 - Strange bee or bee anomaly, possibly a supernatural or unusual event involving bees.
  • 白郎 - Proper name, possibly of a historical or legendary figure.
  • 鬼结婚 - Ghost marriage, a supernatural union between the living and the dead.
  • 三世 - Three lifetimes or three generations, often used in Buddhist or Taoist contexts.
  • 妖诈食 - Devouring by fraud or deceitful consumption, possibly a term for a supernatural act of deception and consumption.
  • 缢致富 - Hanging to become rich, a term that may refer to a tragic or supernatural method of obtaining wealth.
  • 卵怪 - Egg monster or egg demon, a term for a creature born from an egg in a supernatural context.
  • 溺簿 - Drowning ledger or drowning record, possibly a reference to a book or list of drowned people.
  • 产怪 - Birth monster or birth demon, a term for a supernatural being born during childbirth.
  • 守宫 - Lizard guardian or wall lizard, often associated with love and omens in Chinese folklore.
  • 青蛙精 - Toad spirit or frog demon, a common supernatural being in Chinese stories.
  • 钱龙 - Money dragon, a term for a supernatural being associated with wealth.
  • 石珠 - Stone pearl, possibly a magical object or a term for a rare gem.
  • 土中鱼 - Fish in the earth or earth fish, a term for a creature found underground or in unusual places.
  • 蝎虎尾 - Scorpion tiger tail, possibly a name or a term for a creature with scorpion-like features.
  • 焦某 - Generic name, possibly a placeholder for a person.
  • 鬼眼 - Ghost eye, a term for an unusual or supernatural eye.
  • 陈姓 - Chen surname, referring to the surname Chen.
  • 义仓怪 - Abnormality in the public granary, possibly referring to a supernatural event in a charitable grain storage facility.
  • 秦裕 - Proper name, likely of a historical or legendary figure.
  • 蛇异 - Snake anomaly or strange snake, possibly a supernatural or unusual event involving snakes.
  • 猬火 - Hedgehog fire, possibly a term for a strange or magical fire.
  • 鸡异四则 - Four strange chicken events, a reference to four unusual or supernatural events involving chickens.
  • 产妖 - Birth demon or birth spirit, a term for a supernatural being born during childbirth.
  • 羊怪 - Sheep monster or sheep demon, a supernatural being in Chinese folklore.
  • 张杰 - Proper name, likely of a historical or legendary figure.
  • 金化水 - Gold into water, possibly a metaphor or a magical transformation.
  • 尸起 - Rising corpse or rising dead, referring to the resurrection of the dead.
  • 古磁缸 - Ancient magnetic jar or ancient iron jar, possibly a magical object.
  • 优伶某 - A certain actor or a certain performer, referring to a member of the theatrical profession.
  • 河间乙 - Proper name, possibly of a historical or legendary figure.
  • 齐某 - Generic name, possibly a placeholder for a person.
  • 曹商 - Proper name, likely of a historical or legendary figure.
  • 张老殿 - Proper name, possibly a title or place name.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 冤魂 - Common in Chinese folklore and religious beliefs, referring to spirits of the wrongfully dead who may seek justice or vengeance.
  • 二仙 - Often refers to two immortals or deities, such as the Eight Immortals or other legendary figures.
  • 槐仙 - Willow spirit or willow ghost may refer to a specific spirit associated with willow trees, often linked to the Hui tree in Daoist tradition.
  • 张千总 - The term 千总 is a military rank used in the Qing Dynasty, so 张千总 likely refers to a military officer named Zhang.
  • 粤东童 - Refers to someone from Yue Dong (East of Guangdong), a region in southern China.
  • 营弁 - A term from the Qing military system, 营弁 refers to a mid-level military officer.
  • 夙冤 - A concept from Buddhist and Daoist cosmology, referring to karmic debts or past-life grudges.
  • 粥厂 - A zhuo chang (porridge factory) refers to a charitable institution that distributes porridge to the poor, common in Chinese history.
  • 猪龙 - A term that may refer to a mythical creature, possibly a hybrid of a pig and a dragon, or a mischievous spirit.
  • 守宫 - Also known as gecko, a small lizard that is often associated with love and omens in Chinese folklore.
  • 青蛙精 - A common mythological creature in Chinese stories, often portrayed as a mischievous or magical being.
  • 钱龙 - A term for a spirit or creature associated with wealth, often linked to the idea of money dragons in Chinese folklore.
  • 石珠 - May refer to a magical object or a rare gemstone.
  • 土中鱼 - A term for a creature found in the earth, possibly a magical or symbolic fish.
  • 蝎虎尾 - A term that may refer to a creature with features of both a scorpion and a tiger, possibly a mythical being.
  • 义仓 - A yi cang (public granary) was a charitable institution in ancient China that stored grain for distribution during famines.
  • 蛇异 - Refers to unusual or supernatural events involving snakes, which are often associated with both danger and transformation in Chinese folklore.
  • 猬火 - A term that may refer to a strange or magical fire, possibly linked to the hedgehog or a specific myth.
  • 鸡异四则 - Refers to four unusual or supernatural events involving chickens, which may be part of a collection of folktales or anomalies.
  • 产妖 - A term for a supernatural being born during childbirth, often associated with omens or misfortune.
  • 羊怪 - A term for a supernatural being in the form of a sheep or goat, often with magical or malevolent traits.

[8]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 醉茶志怪 - Title of the text, '醉茶' likely refers to a state of intoxication or deep involvement in tea, and '志怪' means 'records of strange things' or 'supernatural tales.'
  • 折獄二則 - Literally 'two cases of breaking the law,' but more accurately 'two cases of judicial decision-making' or 'two judicial stories.'
  • 赴任時 - Means 'when taking office' or 'upon assuming the position.'
  • 投止旅舍 - Means 'to seek lodging at an inn.'
  • 主人殷洽備至 - Means 'the host was extremely hospitable' or 'the host showed great kindness.'
  • 請公寓偏室 - Means 'asked to stay in a side room.'
  • 公嫌隘甚 - Means 'the official found it too cramped' or 'the official thought it was very narrow.'
  • 乃息正廳復室 - Means 'so he rested in the main hall's upstairs room.'
  • 墙壁光泽 - Means 'the walls were glossy' or 'the walls shone.'
  • 设一板床 - Means 'set up a wooden bed.'
  • 四周遮以布幄 - Means 'covered it with cloth curtains.'
  • 遣仆展卧具 - Means 'sent a servant to prepare the bedding.'
  • 息偃在床 - Means 'lay down on the bed.'
  • 二更将尽 - Means 'near the end of the second watch' (a traditional Chinese time division).
  • 烛影凝青 - Means 'the candlelight turned greenish.'
  • 剪之仍暗 - Means 'even when trimmed, it remained dim.'
  • 不之怪也 - Means 'he did not find it strange.'
  • 甫交睫 - Means 'as soon as he closed his eyes.'
  • 觉有物拂面 - Means 'felt something brushing against his face.'
  • 骇而视之 - Means 'startled and looked at it.'
  • 顶格去脸咫尺 - Means 'it was just a few inches away from his face.'

  • 女 - Proper noun referring to a woman, likely a character in the story.
  • 甲 - Proper noun referring to a man, likely a character in the story.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 醉茶子曰 - Colloquial way of quoting a speaker, similar to 'a drunken tea drinker said.'
  • 贪欢忘死 - Literally means 'greedily pursuing pleasure and forgetting death,' suggesting a hedonistic lifestyle.
  • 深于情者，死快于生 - Literally means 'those deeply immersed in emotion find death quicker than life,' implying a deep emotional state that makes death seem preferable.
  • 况生而不死乎 - Literally means 'how much more so if one lives but does not die,' emphasizing the contrast between living and dying.
  • 何愚昧至此哉 - Literally means 'how foolish and ignorant can one be,' expressing strong disapproval.
  • 吾独恨甲之忍 - Literally means 'I alone hate the cruelty of A,' indicating a specific person's cruelty.
  • 明知良药，不使苏服 - Literally means 'knowing the good medicine, he does not make him take it,' suggesting a refusal to help someone who needs it.
  • 自饮之，鹤发童颜，优游岁月 - Literally means 'he drinks it himself, with white hair and a youthful appearance, enjoying his years leisurely,' describing a person who benefits from the medicine.
  • 天龙有知，何不奋雷一击哉 - Literally means 'if the heavenly dragon knew, why not strike with a thunderbolt,' expressing a wish for divine intervention.
  • 张顺者，武定府人 - Literally means 'Zhang Shun, a man from Wuding Prefecture,' introducing the main character.
  • 值寇横恣 - Literally means 'encountering bandits acting wildly,' describing a dangerous situation.
  • 窜身荒野 - Literally means 'fleeing into the wilderness,' indicating a desperate escape.
  • 日暮途穷 - Literally means 'at dusk, at the end of the road,' suggesting a dire situation.
  • 恐为贼获 - Literally means 'fearing capture by the bandits,' expressing fear.
  • 四顾无赴藏匿 - Literally means 'looking around, there is nowhere to hide,' indicating a lack of options.
  • 乃伏乱尸中 - Literally means 'he then hides among the corpses,' describing a desperate hiding place.
  • 云霾月黑 - Literally means 'clouds cover the sky, the moon is dark,' setting a gloomy atmosphere.
  • 悲风四号 - Literally means 'grief-filled winds howl all around,' creating a somber mood.
  • 毛发森森俱竖 - Literally means 'hair stands on end,' describing a state of fear or shock.
  • 忽高处仰见灯光 - Literally means 'suddenly, looking up, he sees lights,' indicating a sudden change in the scene.
  • 一官居中坐，旁列数卤簿 - Literally means 'an official sits in the middle, with several attendants standing around,' describing a formal gathering.
  • 展册唱名 - Literally means 'opening the register and calling names,' indicating a formal process.
  • 便见断手缺臂者蹒跚而上 - Literally means 'he sees people with missing limbs staggering up,' describing a disturbing sight.
  • 须臾点毕，纷纷并倒 - Literally means 'in a moment, the process is complete, and they all fall down,' indicating a quick and dramatic event.
  • 怪风一卷，万象俱无 - Literally means 'a strange wind blows, and everything disappears,' describing a sudden and mysterious event.
  • 顺大恐，伏至晓 - Literally means 'Zhang Shun is greatly afraid, hiding until dawn,' indicating a long period of fear.
  • 贼过如未见者 - Literally means 'the bandits pass by as if they did not see him,' describing a narrow escape.
  • 遂得免 - Literally means 'thus he escapes,' indicating the outcome.
  • 自此懔懔怀刑 - Literally means 'from then on, he is always mindful of the law,' indicating a change in behavior.
  • 恒以南游为戒 - Literally means 'always taking a southern journey as a warning,' suggesting a lesson learned.
  • 会岁大饥，饮食不给 - Literally means 'during a year of great famine, food and drink are insufficient,' describing a difficult time.
  • 丐食入都 - Literally means 'begging for food in the capital,' indicating a desperate situation.
  • 至一官第 - Literally means 'arriving at an official's residence,' describing a new setting.
  • 有老妪抱儿立门外 - Literally means 'an old woman stands outside the door with a child in her arms,' introducing a new character.
  • 儿大啼 - Literally means 'the child cries loudly,' indicating distress.
  • 饲以果饴，啼愈甚 - Literally means 'feeding him with fruit and candy, the crying becomes even louder,' suggesting the child's dissatisfaction.
  • 举头见顺，啼即止，且求顺抱 - Literally means 'lifting his head to see Zhang Shun, the crying stops, and he asks to be held,' indicating a change in the child's behavior.
  • 顺喜抱之，儿悦甚 - Literally means 'Zhang Shun happily holds him, and the child is very happy,' describing a positive interaction.
  • 顺辞欲行，儿又大号 - Literally means 'Zhang Shun tries to leave, and the child cries again,' indicating the child's attachment.
  • 好事仆白于主人 - Literally means 'a kind servant informs the master,' introducing a new character.
  • 主人唤入，视之，丐也 - Literally means 'the master calls him in, looks at him, and sees he is a beggar,' describing the master's reaction.
  • 易其衣履，留宅内，使侍公子 - Literally means 'he changes his clothes and shoes, keeps him in the house, and makes him serve the young master,' indicating a change in Zhang Shun's status.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 醉茶子 - A fictional or allegorical figure, possibly representing a wise or critical voice.
  • 武定府 - A historical prefecture, now part of Shandong Province, China.
  • 寇 - Bandits or rebels, often used in historical contexts to describe lawless groups.
  • 天龙 - A mythical creature, often associated with divine power and intervention.
  • 卤簿 - Attendants or guards, often accompanying officials in formal settings.
  • 南游 - A journey to the south, often used metaphorically to describe a dangerous or undesirable trip.
  • 丐 - A beggar, often used to describe someone in a desperate or low social status.
  • 官第 - An official's residence, indicating a place of authority and wealth.
  • 老妪 - An elderly woman, often used to describe a maternal or nurturing figure.
  • 公子 - A young nobleman, often used to describe a member of the aristocracy.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 順喜出望外 - Indicates that 'Shun was overjoyed beyond expectation.'
  • 得免饥寒 - Means 'escaped from hunger and cold.'
  • 且获赏赍 - Means 'and received rewards.'
  • 曲意逢迎 - Means 'to cater to or flatter.'
  • 举前事以对 - Means 'to recount the previous events.'
  • 苟能守法 - Means 'if one can adhere to the law.'
  • 安能陷于囹圄 - Means 'how could one be imprisoned.'
  • 我力不难出也 - Means 'it would not be difficult for me to release them.'
  • 负荆长跪 - Means 'to kneel with a bundle of thorns on one's back, symbolizing repentance.'
  • 拍案大怒 - Means 'to rage and bang the table.'
  • 痛笞而系于禁 - Means 'to severely whip and imprison.'
  • 不堪其苦 - Means 'unable to bear the suffering.'
  • 忿而坠锁 - Means 'angrily breaking the lock.'
  • 导毙 - Means 'leading to death.'
  • 生死之数 - Means 'the fate of life and death.'
  • 卒难逃也 - Means 'cannot ultimately be avoided.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 工部主政 - Refers to an official position in the Ministry of Works, indicating a high-ranking official.
  • 囹圄 - Refers to prison or jail.
  • 拍案大怒 - A vivid description of anger, often used in classical literature.
  • 禁 - Refers to a prison or detention facility.

Proper Nouns

  • 順 - The name of the servant.
  • 主人 - The master or employer.
  • 公子 - The prince or nobleman.
  • 扬州 - A city in modern-day Jiangsu Province.
  • 扬子江 - Another name for the Yangtze River.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 醉茶子曰 - Formal introduction, likely the author or a speaker addressing the reader.
  • 一见辄喜 - Literally 'to immediately like upon seeing,' an idiomatic expression meaning to feel an instant affinity or pleasure.
  • 祸患即伏于中 - Literally 'disaster and trouble are already lurking inside,' an idiomatic expression meaning that hidden dangers or problems are present even when things seem good.
  • 厚我者未必不祸我 - Literally 'those who treat me well may not necessarily harm me,' an idiomatic expression meaning that even those who are kind or supportive may cause harm.
  • 祸我者未必不福我 - Literally 'those who harm me may not necessarily bring me good fortune,' an idiomatic expression meaning that even those who cause harm may ultimately bring benefits.
  • 横逆之来 - Literally 'the coming of adversity,' an idiomatic expression referring to unexpected difficulties or challenges.
  • 平情处之 - Literally 'to treat it with calmness,' an idiomatic expression meaning to handle a situation with composure and equanimity.
  • 安知非如塞翁之失马哉 - Literally 'how can we know it is not like the story of the old man of the border losing his horse?' An idiomatic expression referencing the parable of the old man of the border (塞翁失马), which illustrates the idea that what seems like misfortune may lead to unexpected good fortune.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 前缘 - A concept from Buddhist and Daoist thought, referring to karmic connections or fated relationships.
  • 塞翁之失马 - A well-known parable from the *Huainanzi* (淮南子), which tells the story of an old man who loses his horse but ultimately gains more than he loses. The story is often used to illustrate the idea that misfortune may lead to unexpected good fortune.

Proper Nouns

  • 醉茶子 - The author or speaker, possibly a fictional or historical figure. The name suggests a connection to tea culture or Daoist philosophy.
  • 塞翁 - The old man of the border, a character from the *Huainanzi* who is the subject of the parable about losing a horse.

[17]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 嗜酒病噎，飲食不進 - Compound expression describing a condition of excessive drinking that has led to a blockage in the throat or esophagus, preventing the person from eating or drinking.
  • 群醫束手 - Classical idiom meaning 'the physicians all gave up' or 'the doctors were at a loss,' indicating that no one could treat the illness.
  • 已濒于死 - Formal expression meaning 'already on the verge of death.'
  • 日祷于神，罔效 - Classical construction: 'daily prayed to the gods, but without effect.' The phrase *罔效* is a negated result.
  • 殆木作工也 - *殆* is used as a speculative particle, meaning 'probably' or 'likely.' *木作工* refers to a craftsman or carpenter.
  • 令亲冯某，遣予视疾 - Polite form of address: 'Your relative, Mr. Feng, has sent me to visit the illness.' *令亲* is a respectful way of referring to someone else's relative.
  • 病革矣 - *病革* is a classical expression meaning 'the illness is grave' or 'the condition is critical.'
  • 身后事切求援手 - Formal expression meaning 'urgent request for help with the matters after death,' i.e., funeral arrangements or last rites.
  • 请勿迟疑 - Polite command meaning 'please do not hesitate.'
  • 索纸笔书方授之 - Compound action: 'asked for paper and brush to write the prescription and gave it to her.'
  • 此药三剂，保必愈矣 - Confident statement: 'This medicine in three doses will definitely cure it.'
  • 买药进之 - Concise expression meaning 'bought the medicine and gave it to him.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 袁某，邑之李富德庄人 - *袁某* is a generic name (Mr. Yuan), *邑* refers to the county or local area, *李富德庄* is the name of the village or hamlet. This establishes the setting in a rural Chinese community.
  • 妻李氏最贤 - *李氏* is a common surname, and *最贤* means 'most virtuous,' highlighting the wife's moral character in a classical Confucian context.
  • 祷于神 - Refers to a traditional Chinese practice of praying to deities or ancestors for healing, common in pre-modern Chinese society.
  • 木作工 - A term for a carpenter or woodworker, a common trade in classical Chinese society.
  • 方诘之 - *方* here refers to a prescription or formula, and *诘之* means 'to question or inquire about it,' suggesting that the wife sought the opinion of a scholar or teacher.
  • 塾师范某 - *塾师* is a private tutor or teacher in a small school, and *范某* is a generic name (Mr. Fan), indicating a local scholar or intellectual.

Proper Nouns

  • 袁某 - Generic name; the character's name is not specified, so it is left as 'Mr. Yuan' or 'Mr. Yuan' in translation.
  • 李富德庄 - A village or hamlet; rendered as 'Li Fude Village' or 'Li Fude Hamlet.'
  • 李氏 - The wife, whose surname is Li; rendered as 'Lady Li' or 'Mrs. Li.'
  • 冯某 - A relative of the patient; rendered as 'Mr. Feng.'
  • 范某 - The scholar or tutor; rendered as 'Mr. Fan.'
  • 高商家 - A merchant in the nearby village; rendered as 'Mr. Gao's shop' or 'the Gao family's shop.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 病者腸腹雷鳴 - Describes a patient's abdomen making loud rumbling sounds, indicating severe gastrointestinal distress.
  • 片刻三遺矢 - Means the patient defecated three times in a very short period.
  • 頓索食飲 - Indicates the patient suddenly craved food and drink.
  • 與以湯餌 - Means to give the patient soup and medicine.
  • 居然下咽 - Suggests the patient unexpectedly managed to swallow the food or medicine.
  • 再服兩劑，疾大愈 - Means after taking two more doses, the patient's condition significantly improved.
  • 吾其遇和、緩乎？ - A rhetorical question asking if the patient has encountered a skilled doctor like He or Huan.
  • 何其術之神也？ - Exclaims about the doctor's miraculous skill.
  • 乃詣馮致謝 - Means to go to Feng to express gratitude.
  • 馮茫然不解 - Indicates Feng is confused and does not understand.
  • 哀告以故 - Means to explain the reason with sorrow.
  • 相與猜異 - Suggests mutual speculation and disagreement.
  • 因辭馮詣高 - Means to leave Feng and go to Gao.
  • 高方大興土木 - Indicates Gao is building something grand.
  • 鸠工如云 - Means there are many workers gathered.
  • 問工而醫者誰？ - Asks which worker is a doctor.
  • 眾言其無 - Means everyone says there is no doctor among the workers.
  • 诧异而返 - Indicates returning in surprise.
  • 告其故於妻 - Means to tell the wife the reason.
  • 妻劝访诸逆旅 - Means the wife suggests visiting inns.
  • 袁新瘥体倦 - Indicates Yuan is weak and tired after recovering.
  • 店主人方启关 - Means the innkeeper is just opening the door.
  • 延之入 - Means to invite him in.
  • 詢客有木匠而醫者耶？ - Asks if any guest is both a carpenter and a doctor.
  • 曰：“不知。” - Means 'I don't know.'
  • 袁乃遍视诸客 - Means Yuan looks at all the guests.
  • 悉非其人 - Means none of them are the right person.
  • 至院西隅有静室 - Means there is a quiet room in the west corner of the courtyard.
  • 内奉鲁班神像 - Means the room has a statue of Lu Ban, the god of carpentry.
  • 厥貌惟肖 - Means the statue looks very lifelike.
  • 恍然顿悟 - Means suddenly realizing something.
  • 顶礼毕 - Means to finish bowing in reverence.
  • 归，遣妻亦往礼之 - Means to return home and send the wife to pay respects as well.
  • 由是鲁班之灵大著 - Means the spirit of Lu Ban became very famous.
  • 焚香祈福者肩摩毂击矣 - Means many people come to burn incense and pray for blessings, so crowded that their shoulders and wheels touch.
  • 后凡有病噎者 - Means later, anyone with throat problems.
  • 服其方无不立效 - Means taking the prescription always works immediately.
  • 无何，邻村有张髡者 - Means not long after, there was a bald man named Zhang from a neighboring village.
  • 患是病 - Means suffering from this illness.
  • 遣人索方于袁 - Means sending someone to ask Yuan for the prescription.
  • 袁他出 - Means Yuan was out.
  • 其妻捡方付之 - Means Yuan's wife found the prescription and gave it to the messenger.
  • 告使者曰：“神方只此一纸，宝藏之勿失也。” - Means telling the messenger, 'The divine prescription is just this one paper; treasure it and do not lose it.'
  • 使藏而去 - Means the messenger stored it and left.
  • 至半里许，遽中暑 - Means halfway, the messenger suddenly got sunstroke.
  • 踣于路 - Means fell down on the road.
  • 舁至家卒 - Means was carried home and died.
  • 方遂不可问矣 - Means the prescription was lost.
  • 未几髡亦毙 - Means not long after, the bald man also died.
  • 始悟神言不治秃发者之有因也 - Means they finally realized why the god said it wouldn't work for bald people.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 和、缓 - Refers to two famous doctors in Chinese history, He and Huan, known for their miraculous healing skills.
  • 鲁班 - The legendary figure Lu Ban, considered the god of carpentry in Chinese culture.
  • 焚香祈福 - A common practice in Chinese culture where people burn incense and pray for blessings or protection.

Proper Nouns

  • 袁 - The narrator's name.
  • 冯 - The name of the person the narrator goes to thank.
  • 高 - The name of the person building the grand project.
  • 张髡 - The name of the bald man from the neighboring village.
  • 和、缓 - The names of two famous doctors.
  • 鲁班 - The legendary figure Lu Ban.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 醉茶子曰 - An idiomatic opening phrase meaning 'The Tea Drinker said.' It is a playful and somewhat obscure reference, possibly a pseudonym or a style of expression used by the author.
  • 公输子 - Refers to Gongshu Ban, a famous craftsman and inventor from the Spring and Autumn period known for his skills in carpentry and mechanical engineering.
  • 刻术为人、为鸢，悉能飞走 - Describes Gongshu Ban's ability to create lifelike figures and mechanical devices, such as birds that could fly.
  • 未闻以医传 - Means 'I have not heard of him being known for medicine.'
  • 巫医小术 - Refers to 'minor magical and medical practices,' often used pejoratively.
  • 特古人之余事乎 - Means 'merely the leftover skills of ancient people,' suggesting a dismissive attitude towards these practices.
  • 而今之医士 - Refers to 'modern-day physicians.'
  • 投药卤葬 - Means 'to prescribe medicine carelessly,' implying a lack of skill or professionalism.
  • 与运斤纵斧者无殊 - Means 'no different from those who wield axes recklessly,' further emphasizing the lack of skill.
  • 是不但有愧卢、扁 - Means 'this is not only a disgrace to Lu and Bian,' referring to the legendary physicians Lu Ban and Bian Que.
  • 直弄斧班门耳 - Means 'it's like showing off an axe in front of the master craftsman,' a metaphor for demonstrating one's skills in the presence of an expert.
  • 而况执斧者公然为医哉 - Means 'and what about those who openly claim to be doctors while holding an axe?'
  • 洵可叹矣 - Means 'truly it is a pity,' expressing a sense of regret or disappointment.
  • 王建屏 - Refers to Wang Jianping, a noble family member from the Jin dynasty who moved to Tianjin.
  • 从其祖迁居于津 - Means 'followed his grandfather to move to Tianjin.'
  • 父母旱逝 - Means 'his parents died early,' indicating a tragic personal history.
  • 年十八，与邑之杂货商为伙友 - Means 'at eighteen, he became a partner with a local general store merchant.'
  • 肆后厦屋五楹 - Means 'the store had a five-bay hall behind it.'
  • 王独后复室 - Means 'Wang had a separate room at the back.'
  • 更深扃户，煨酒自饮 - Means 'at night, he locked the door and warmed wine to drink alone.'
  • 忽布帘一展，有女子自外入 - Means 'suddenly, a curtain was drawn, and a woman entered from outside.'
  • 缟素衣裳 - Means 'wearing white and plain clothes,' indicating a modest and simple appearance.
  • 面无脂粉 - Means 'her face had no makeup,' emphasizing her natural beauty.
  • 虚鬟笼雾 - Means 'her hair was loosely coiled, as if veiled in mist,' describing her hairstyle.
  • 腻颊凝花 - Means 'her cheeks were smooth and flower-like,' describing her skin.
  • 淡雅别饶风韵 - Means 'she was elegant and had a unique charm,' emphasizing her grace and beauty.
  • 王知其狐仙，毛发几竖 - Means 'Wang knew she was a fox spirit, and his hair stood on end,' indicating his fear and surprise.
  • 谁见尔敬我耶 - Means 'Who has seen you showing respect to me?'
  • 痴郎，我未见有敬妇者 - Means 'Foolish man, I have never seen anyone show respect to a woman,' indicating the woman's playful and confident demeanor.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • Gongshu Ban, a renowned craftsman and inventor from the Spring and Autumn period.
  • Lu Ban and Bian Que, legendary physicians in Chinese history.
  • Wang Jianping, a noble family member from the Jin dynasty, reflecting the social hierarchy and mobility of the time.
  • General store merchants, common in traditional Chinese society.
  • Fox spirits, a common motif in Chinese folklore and mythology, often depicted as seductive and mischievous.

  • 醉茶子 - A playful or pseudonymous title used by the author.
  • 公输子 - Gongshu Ban, a historical figure.
  • 卢、扁 - Lu Ban and Bian Que, legendary physicians.
  • 王建屏 - A historical figure from the Jin dynasty.
  • 津 - Tianjin, a city in northern China.
  • 狐仙 - Fox spirits, a common motif in Chinese folklore.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 予固未娶 - I have indeed not married; *固* emphasizes certainty or firmness.
  • 何便诬我为敬妇 - Why would you so easily accuse me of being a virtuous wife? *便* suggests ease or casualness, *诬* means to falsely accuse or defame.
  • 痴哉！痴哉！ - An exclamation of disbelief or mock pity, meaning 'How foolish! How foolish!'
  • 缘何不娶 - 'Why haven't you married?' *缘何* is a classical way of asking 'why.'
  • 家道赤贫 - 'The family is extremely poor.' *赤贫* is an idiom meaning 'extreme poverty.'
  • 谁肯媒妁 - 'Who would be willing to act as a matchmaker?' *媒妁* refers to matchmakers or the act of arranging marriage.
  • 我为媒，为尔择一佳妇，愿乎？ - 'I will be your matchmaker and choose a fine wife for you. Will you agree?' *愿乎* is a classical way of asking for consent or willingness.
  • 麻面兔唇，且眇一目 - 'She has a rough face, a rabbit's lip, and is blind in one eye.' *兔唇* refers to a cleft lip, and *眇一目* means one eye is blind or impaired.
  • 君如愿之，是君白首之偶 - 'If you are willing, she will be your lifelong partner.' *白首之偶* is a classical expression meaning 'life partner until old age.'
  • 不可！不可！ - A repeated exclamation of refusal or disapproval.
  • 必美如姐姐者，方可为之 - 'Only someone as beautiful as my sister can be considered.' *方可为之* means 'can be considered' or 'can be chosen.'
  • 俗云癞头蛙想吃天鹅，不亦难乎 - 'The saying goes: a癞头蛙 (a frog with a bald head, a humorous or derogatory term) wants to eat a swan—how difficult is that?' This is a proverbial expression used to mock unrealistic expectations.
  • 我诚不敢妄想 - 'I truly dare not have wild fantasies.' *诚* means 'truly' or 'indeed.'
  • 谚云：野老食蝗，飞来口福，不劳君子好逑耳 - 'The saying goes: a wild old man eats locusts, and it comes as a blessing—no need for a gentleman to seek a virtuous match.' *野老食蝗* is a proverbial expression, and *好逑* comes from the *Book of Poetry*, meaning 'a good match.'
  • 君非学究，何太腐气？ - 'You are not a scholar, yet you are so overly conventional!' *学究* refers to a pedantic scholar or bookworm; *腐气* means 'stuffy' or 'overly traditional.'
  • 岂日居市井，尚未忘之乎者也耶？ - 'How is it that you live in the marketplace yet have not forgotten it?' This is a rhetorical question with a classical structure, suggesting that the speaker has not moved away from their former life or values.
  • 实告君，妾与君有前缘，故来燕好 - 'Let me tell you honestly, I and you have a karmic connection from before, hence I have come to be with you.' *前缘* means 'karmic connection from past lives' or 'fate from before,' and *燕好* refers to a harmonious union or marriage.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 敬妇 - 'Virtuous wife,' a classical term used to describe a woman of moral integrity, often in the context of Confucian ideals of wifely virtue.
  • 媒妁 - Matchmakers or the act of arranging marriages, a key institution in traditional Chinese society.
  • 麻面兔唇 - A description of physical deformities; *麻面* means 'rough face' or 'scarred face,' and *兔唇* is a cleft lip.
  • 白首之偶 - A classical expression meaning 'life partner until old age,' emphasizing a lifelong marriage.
  • 癞头蛙想吃天鹅 - A proverbial expression, likely of folk origin, used to mock someone's unrealistic or foolish desire.
  • 野老食蝗 - A proverbial expression, possibly from folk sayings or poetry, meaning that even a poor or lowly person may receive unexpected blessings.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 貫胸破腹者枕籍其上 - Means 'those with their chests and bellies pierced lie piled up on top of them.' Describes a gruesome scene.

  • 惶恐，幾不能步 - Means 'fearful, almost unable to walk.' Conveys the extreme fear.
  • 雜眾中，立殿檐下 - Means 'standing among the crowd under the eaves of the hall.' Indicates a crowded and tense environment.
  • 俄聞堂鼓訇动，响若雷霆 - Means 'soon hearing the hall drum booming, sounding like thunder.' Describes a sudden and loud noise.
  • 披坚执锐人高声齐吼 - Means 'those wearing armor and holding weapons shout loudly in unison.' Depicts a scene of military readiness.
  • 旌旗披拂中 - Means 'in the fluttering of banners.' Sets the scene of a grand and ceremonial event.
  • 垂冕彩服 - Means 'wearing a ceremonial crown and colorful robe.' Describes the appearance of the king.
  • 南面端坐 - Means 'sitting facing south in a dignified manner.' Indicates the king's position of authority.
  • 上下人众一齐拜舞 - Means 'all the people above and below bow and dance together.' Describes a formal ritual.
  • 虎首人身 - Means 'with a tiger's head and a human body.' Describes a mythical or supernatural figure.
  • 奔上跪献方策 - Means 'rushing up to kneel and present a square strategy.' Indicates a formal presentation.
  • 弃簿于地 - Means 'throwing the book on the ground.' Shows anger or frustration.
  • 万声号呼，乱如鼎沸 - Means 'ten thousand voices shouting, chaotic like boiling water.' Describes a scene of chaos and disorder.

  • 欲出而王已失所在 - Means 'wanting to leave but the king has already disappeared.' Indicates a sudden and mysterious disappearance.
  • 盖死去已二日矣 - Means 'it seems he has been dead for two days.' Suggests a dream or hallucination.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 水车来往道旁千万计 - Vivid description of a bustling scene with thousands of water wheels coming and going by the roadside.
  • 锣声人声相应 - Describes a lively atmosphere with the sound of gongs and people echoing each other.
  • 旌旗烂漫如云锦 - Metaphor comparing colorful flags to a brocade of clouds, emphasizing visual splendor.
  • 香烟喷溢，高上青霄 - Describes incense smoke rising high into the sky, symbolizing spiritual or religious fervor.
  • 夜则灯火遍野，远近繁杂如密星 - Paints a picture of a nighttime scene with numerous lights spread across the fields, far and near, as dense as stars.
  • 华盖彩舆停道左，皆显贵之妻妾也 - Describes colorful carriages and umbrellas stopping on the left side of the road, indicating they belong to wealthy or noble women.
  • 僧揭舆帘向美人洒土唾津，或摩面拭颐，笑云：“愈矣，愈矣。” - Describes a monk lifting the curtain of a carriage, sprinkling dirt and spittle on a beautiful woman, or rubbing her face and cheeks, saying, 'Healed, healed,' suggesting a superstitious or ritualistic act.
  • 即令舁归 - Means 'then they are carried back,' indicating the conclusion of the ritual.
  • 有待半晌不得施治者，即闻轿内嘤嘤娇啼，泣曰“悔罪”，以为活佛不齿 - Describes those who wait for a long time without receiving treatment, hearing soft cries from inside the carriage, and crying out 'repentance,' believing that the living Buddha disapproves of them.

  • 生惊欲号 - The man was about to cry out in fear – a moment of impending danger or fear.

  • 命如纸薄，谁复见怜 - My life is as thin as paper, who will ever pity me? – a poetic expression of fragility and sorrow.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 醉茶子曰 - Classical opening introducing the speaker, likely a literary or philosophical commentator; '醉茶子' translates to 'Drunken Tea-Drinker,' a pseudonym or nickname, possibly indicating a scholar or writer with a penchant for tea and a poetic disposition.
  • 离魂倩女 - Reference to a 'soul-separated beautiful maiden,' a common motif in Chinese literature, especially in tales of love and supernatural elements.
  • 信有之耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Is this truly the case?' or 'Is this true?'
  • 一念钟情 - 'A single thought of deep affection' or 'a sudden, intense love at first sight.'
  • 百端魔障 - 'A hundred kinds of obstacles or hindrances,' often used metaphorically to describe spiritual or emotional trials.
  • 砒毒鸠酒 - 'Poisoned with arsenic and wine' or 'poisoned with arsenic and �鸩 (a type of toxic bird used in poison).'
  • 几杀其身 - 'Almost killed her body' or 'nearly took her life.'
  • 设非贤能孝姑，何以得吉神拥护哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'If it were not for the virtuous and filial daughter-in-law, how could she have received the protection of benevolent spirits?'
  • 弱冠入泮 - Term from the Confucian education system; '弱冠' refers to a man turning 20, the age of adulthood, and '入泮' means to enter the school or academy (泮宫, the school of the county).
  • 世家争婚之，父俱未许 - 'Prominent families vied to marry him, but his father did not agree to any of them.'
  • 适狂盗犯境 - 'At the time when violent bandits invaded the region.'
  • 土枭乘势扰乱 - 'Local bandits took advantage of the situation to cause chaos.' '土枭' can be translated as 'local bandits' or 'rural outlaws.'
  • 白昼御人 - 'To attack people during the day.' '御' here is used in a military or violent sense, meaning to confront or attack.
  • 举县老弱奔窜 - 'All the elderly and weak in the county fled in panic.'
  • 生家积粮为村人掠去 - 'The family’s stored grain was taken by the villagers.' This may be a misreading or an ironic statement, as it implies that the villagers, who were presumably in need, looted the family's supplies.
  • 荣携眷避于上谷 - 'Rong took his family and fled to Shanggu.' '上谷' is a historical region, now part of modern Hebei province.
  • 中表亲朱某 - 'A cousin from the maternal or paternal side, Mr. Zhu.' In Chinese, '中表亲' refers to relatives by marriage or extended family.
  • 是处富绅 - 'This place's wealthy gentry' or 'a rich local landowner.'
  • 为生家赁庑分廪，供给勿衰 - 'Provided shelter and food for the family without fail.' '赁庑' means to rent a house or provide shelter, and '分廪' refers to the distribution of grain or food supplies.
  • 留荣于其家，襄办村团 - 'Kept Rong in his home to help organize the village defense force.'
  • 月给薪水 - 'Monthly salary or stipend.'
  • 荣甚感之 - 'Rong was very grateful to him.'
  • 生因客居仓卒，诵读辄疏 - 'Because he was a guest and in a rush, his reading became infrequent.' '仓卒' means in haste or in a hurried manner.
  • 日惟散步村外释闷 - 'Each day, he only walked outside the village to relieve his boredom.' '释闷' means 'to relieve boredom' or 'to dispel melancholy.'
  • 王妻三十许，风致姣丽，不类村妇 - 'Wang’s wife, about thirty years old, was graceful and beautiful, unlike a typical village woman.' '风致姣丽' means 'graceful and beautiful.'
  • 有女名柳儿，貌美尤过其母 - 'She had a daughter named Liu'er, whose beauty surpassed her mother’s.'
  • 尝随母碾米于比邻，日凡三四往，必过生门 - 'She often accompanied her mother to grind rice at a neighboring house, and each time she went there three or four times a day, she would pass by the scholar’s gate.' '比邻' means 'neighboring house' or 'next-door neighbor.'
  • 生立门外，女携箕帚自对面来 - 'The scholar stood outside the gate, and the girl came from the opposite side carrying a broom and a winnowing basket.' '箕帚' are tools used in housework or agricultural labor.
  • 裙布荆钗，殊无艳饰 - 'Wearing plain clothes and simple hairpins, she had no extravagant adornments.' '裙布荆钗' is a phrase used to describe a woman of modest means or a simple, virtuous woman.
  • 而发盘高髻，秀眉在骨 - 'But her hair was arranged in a high bun, and her elegant eyebrows were clearly defined on her forehead.' '发盘高髻' refers to a traditional hairstyle for women, and '秀眉在骨' poetically describes a woman’s facial features.
  • 生不禁神荡，目送女去远，始返身归 - 'He could not help but be entranced; he watched her go far away before turning back.'
  • 由是冥想梦寐都萦，早起不暇洗漱，即俟诸门外 - 'From then on, his thoughts and dreams were filled with her; he woke up too early to bother washing, and immediately waited outside her gate.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 醉茶子 - A pseudonym or nickname, possibly used by the author to give himself a literary or philosophical identity. Similar to 'Drunkard of Tea' or 'Drunken Tea-Drinker,' it may be a satirical or poetic name.
  • 倩女 - A term used in classical Chinese literature to describe a beautiful and often tragic female figure, often associated with stories of love and ghostly or supernatural elements.
  • 离魂 - A common motif in Chinese literature, especially in the genre of *xianxia* (immortal tales) or *chuanqi* (tales of strange events). It refers to the soul of a woman being separated from her body, often due to love or tragedy.
  • 砒毒鸠酒 - 'Arsenic and wine' or 'poisoned wine.' '鸠' (a type of bird) was traditionally used in poison, and this phrase is a reference to the myth of the *jiu* bird whose feathers were used to make poison.
  • 孝姑 - A term for a virtuous and filial daughter-in-law. In traditional Chinese society, the daughter-in-law was expected to be filial and loyal to her husband’s family.
  • 弱冠入泮 - A term from the Confucian education system. A man who had reached the age of 20 (weak crown, 弱冠) would enter the county school (泮宫), marking the beginning of his formal education and entry into the scholar-official class.
  • 世家 - A term for prominent or aristocratic families, often those with generations of scholarly or official lineage.
  • 狂盗犯境 - 'Rampant bandits invading the region.' This refers to a period of lawlessness or banditry in a local area.
  • 土枭 - A term for local bandits or rural outlaws. It may also have a mythological or symbolic meaning.
  • 上谷 - A historical region in northern China, located in present-day Hebei province. It was known for its strategic location and military significance.
  • 中表亲 - A term for relatives by marriage or extended family, often from the mother’s or father’s side.
  • 富绅 - A wealthy local gentry or landowner, who often played a key role in local governance and social affairs.
  • 赁庑分廪 - 'Renting a house and sharing grain supplies.' This phrase refers to providing shelter and food to a family in need.
  • 村团 - A local defense force or militia, often organized by the gentry in times of banditry or war.
  • 薪水 - In this context, it refers to a stipend or monthly allowance, not the modern meaning of 'salary.'
  • 仓卒 - 'In haste' or 'in a rush,' often used to describe someone who is busy or in a difficult situation.
  • 风致姣丽 - 'Graceful and beautiful,' a poetic way of describing a woman’s elegance and charm.
  • 比邻 - 'Neighboring house' or 'next-door neighbor.'
  • 箕帚 - A broom and winnowing basket, tools used in housework or agricultural labor.
  • 冥想梦寐都萦 - 'His thoughts and dreams were filled with her.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 醉茶子曰 - A phrase where '醉茶子' likely refers to a person who enjoys tea or is associated with tea culture. It can be translated as 'The Tea-Drunk One said' or 'The Tea-Enthusiast said.'
  • 數百年之美材 - '數百年' means 'for several hundred years,' and '美材' refers to 'fine timber' or 'excellent material.' Together, it means 'fine timber that has been around for several hundred years.'
  • 倪遽以賤價購之 - '倪遽' likely refers to a person or family named Ni who acted hastily. '以賤價購之' means 'purchased it at a low price.' The phrase can be translated as 'Ni hastily purchased it at a low price.'
  • 謂非倪氏之福也得乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'How could it not be a blessing for the Ni family?' The phrase can be translated as 'It cannot be said that it is not a blessing for the Ni family.'
  • 鄉愚无知 - '鄉愚' refers to 'rural fools' or 'simple country folk,' and '无知' means 'ignorant.' Together, it means 'rural people who are ignorant.'
  • 遂受其欺 - Means 'thus fell victim to his deceit.'
  • 且以百千为暴富 - '且以百千' means 'and with a hundred thousand,' and '为暴富' means 'became suddenly wealthy.' The phrase can be translated as 'and thus became suddenly wealthy.'
  • 此其所以为乡愚也 - Means 'This is why they are rural fools.'
  • 宅仙 - Refers to a 'household deity' or 'spirit of the house.'
  • 昔客山右 - '昔' means 'formerly,' '客' means 'guest,' and '山右' refers to 'Shanxi Province' (literally 'the right of the mountains'). The phrase can be translated as 'Formerly, when I was a guest in Shanxi.'
  • 寓居巨绅王氏厅次 - '寓居' means 'to reside as a guest,' '巨绅' refers to a 'wealthy and influential person,' '王氏' means 'the Wang family,' and '厅次' refers to 'the hall of the residence.' The phrase can be translated as 'residing as a guest in the hall of the wealthy Wang family.'
  • 夜半朦胧间 - '夜半' means 'midnight,' and '朦胧间' means 'in a hazy or dreamlike state.' The phrase can be translated as 'in the hazy state of midnight.'
  • 闻柝声繁碎 - '闻' means 'to hear,' '柝声' refers to 'the sound of a wooden clapper,' and '繁碎' means 'repeated and fragmented.' The phrase can be translated as 'heard the repeated and fragmented sound of a wooden clapper.'
  • 俄闻声自远而近 - '俄' means 'soon,' '闻声' means 'heard the sound,' '自远而近' means 'from far to near.' The phrase can be translated as 'soon heard the sound coming from far to near.'
  • 渐至房门 - '渐至' means 'gradually approached,' and '房门' means 'the door of the room.' The phrase can be translated as 'gradually approached the door of the room.'
  • 忽在堂中 - '忽' means 'suddenly,' and '堂中' means 'in the hall.' The phrase can be translated as 'suddenly appeared in the hall.'
  • 惊视 - Means 'to look in shock.'
  • 见一老叟高三四尺 - '见' means 'to see,' '一老叟' means 'an old man,' '高三四尺' means 'tall about three or four feet.' The phrase can be translated as 'saw an old man tall about three or four feet.'
  • 手持小木柝敲击未已 - '手持' means 'holding in hand,' '小木柝' means 'a small wooden clapper,' '敲击' means 'to tap,' '未已' means 'not yet finished.' The phrase can be translated as 'holding a small wooden clapper, tapping it not yet finished.'
  • 徐徐至屋隅而没 - '徐徐' means 'slowly,' '至' means 'to,' '屋隅' means 'the corner of the room,' '而没' means 'and disappeared.' The phrase can be translated as 'slowly went to the corner of the room and disappeared.'
  • 约一更许 - '约' means 'about,' '一更' refers to 'one watch of the night' (a unit of time in ancient China), '许' means 'or so.' The phrase can be translated as 'about one watch of the night or so.'
  • 隐隐自墙隅出 - '隐隐' means 'faintly,' '自' means 'from,' '墙隅' means 'the corner of the wall.' The phrase can be translated as 'faintly appeared from the corner of the wall.'
  • 柝声丁丁然 - '柝声' means 'the sound of a wooden clapper,' '丁丁然' means 'tapping sound.' The phrase can be translated as 'with a tapping sound.'
  • 缓步至门隙 - '缓步' means 'slowly walked,' '至' means 'to,' '门隙' means 'the gap in the door.' The phrase can be translated as 'slowly walked to the gap in the door.'
  • 扁身而出 - '扁身' means 'slightly bent body,' '而出' means 'and went out.' The phrase can be translated as 'slightly bent body and went out.'
  • 计一夜凡五出入 - '计' means 'calculated,' '一夜' means 'one night,' '凡' means 'altogether,' '五出入' means 'five entries and exits.' The phrase can be translated as 'calculated that in one night there were altogether five entries and exits.'
  • 意其按更筹也 - '意' means 'thought,' '其' means 'it,' '按更筹' means 'to keep track of the night watches,' '也' is a particle indicating a statement. The phrase can be translated as 'thought that it was keeping track of the night watches.'
  • 客卧榻上 - '客' means 'the guest,' '卧' means 'lying,' '榻上' means 'on the bed.' The phrase can be translated as 'the guest lying on the bed.'
  • 叟若未之见 - '叟' means 'the old man,' '若' means 'as if,' '未之见' means 'did not see him.' The phrase can be translated as 'as if the old man did not see him.'
  • 客亦若未之见也者 - '客' means 'the guest,' '亦' means 'also,' '若未之见' means 'as if did not see him,' '也者' is a particle indicating a statement. The phrase can be translated as 'the guest also as if did not see him.'
  • 以其不扰人 - '以' means 'because,' '其' means 'it,' '不扰人' means 'did not disturb people.' The phrase can be translated as 'because it did not disturb people.'
  • 姑听之 - '姑' means 'temporarily,' '听之' means 'to listen to it.' The phrase can be translated as 'temporarily listened to it.'
  • 次日白于主人 - '次日' means 'the next day,' '白' means 'to inform,' '于' means 'to,' '主人' means 'the host.' The phrase can be translated as 'the next day informed the host.'
  • 始知其为宅仙 - '始' means 'then,' '知' means 'knew,' '其' means 'it,' '为宅仙' means 'was a household deity.' The phrase can be translated as 'then knew that it was a household deity.'
  • 乃迁客于他室 - '乃' means 'then,' '迁' means 'to move,' '客' means 'the guest,' '于' means 'to,' '他室' means 'another room.' The phrase can be translated as 'then moved the guest to another room.'
  • 果谁使之 - '果' means 'indeed,' '谁' means 'who,' '使之' means 'made it.' The phrase can be translated as 'indeed who made it.'
  • 尝见人家兴衰 - '尝' means 'once,' '见' means 'saw,' '人家' means 'household,' '兴衰' means 'prosperity and decline.' The phrase can be translated as 'once saw the prosperity and decline of households.'
  • 仙即从中以裒益之 - '仙' means 'the deity,' '即' means 'then,' '从中' means 'from within,' '以' means 'to,' '裒益之' means 'to reduce or increase.' The phrase can be translated as 'the deity then from within reduced or increased it.'

  • 以故予伯高祖惠远公举囤中粮，悉济乡邻 - Literal meaning: 'therefore, my great-great-grandfather, Huaiyuan, raised grain from the granary to help all the neighbors.' Describes a charitable act.
  • 盖不贪其贿，恐他日之消算酷焉 - Literal meaning: 'because he did not covet their bribes, fearing a severe reckoning in the future.' Explains the motivation behind the act.
  • 左邻张氏，除夕煮水角，沸汤滚滚，以物捞取，则乌有矣 - Literal meaning: 'the left neighbor, Zhang, boiled water dumplings on New Year's Eve, the boiling water was rolling, and when scooped with a tool, there were none.' Describes a mysterious disappearance of the dumplings.
  • 而予家釜中之水角则多倍于常，细辨半张氏之物 - Literal meaning: 'but the water dumplings in my family's pot were several times more than usual, and upon careful examination, they were half from Zhang's.' Indicates a supernatural transfer.
  • 不知何以投入于予家釜中 - Literal meaning: 'it is unknown how they ended up in my family's pot.' Expresses uncertainty about the cause.
  • 诸如此类甚多 - Literal meaning: 'there were many such incidents.' Indicates the frequency of similar events.
  • 后张氏家落，遂迁居焉 - Literal meaning: 'later, the Zhang family declined and moved away.' Describes the outcome for the Zhang family.
  • 又城中朱氏，居邻卖酱者之家 - Literal meaning: 'another case in the city involves the Zhu family, who lived next to a house that sold soy sauce.' Introduces another incident.
  • 其厨中盐豉醯酱等物，恒用之不竭 - Literal meaning: 'the items in their kitchen, such as salt, fermented beans, vinegar, and soy sauce, were always used without running out.' Describes an inexplicable abundance.
  • 皆仙取邻家物暗为益之 - Literal meaning: 'all due to the immortal taking things from the neighbor's house and secretly benefiting them.' Attributes the abundance to supernatural intervention.
  • 及其衰也，物不食而自尽 - Literal meaning: 'when they declined, the items were not eaten but vanished on their own.' Describes the reversal of fortune.
  • 计所失浮于所得 - Literal meaning: 'the losses outweighed the gains.' Summarizes the net negative outcome.
  • 噫，果何心哉？ - Literal meaning: 'Alas, what was their intention?' Expresses a rhetorical question about the motives.
  • 盖天下事以贪婪聚者，必以暴殄亡 - Literal meaning: 'the world's affairs that accumulate through greed will inevitably perish through wastefulness.' Offers a moral lesson.
  • 权虽在仙而亦在人 - Literal meaning: 'although the power lies with the immortal, it also lies with humans.' Suggests a balance between supernatural and human agency.
  • 甚矣贪之一字，近于贫也 - Literal meaning: 'how great the word 'greed' is, it is close to poverty.' Emphasizes the negative consequences of greed.
  • 非其有而取之 - Literal meaning: 'taking what does not belong to one.' Describes unethical behavior.
  • 君子尚嫌其非义，况于耶祟鬼蜮中求富哉！ - Literal meaning: 'even a gentleman would consider it unjust, let alone seeking wealth through the tricks of ghosts and demons!' Condemns unethical means of acquiring wealth.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 癸酉秋七月夜 - Specific date and time in the Chinese calendar system: '癸酉' is a stem-branch combination, '秋' means autumn, and '七月夜' means the night of the seventh month.
  • 暴风雷雨 - Literal phrase meaning 'violent wind and thunderstorm,' describing a severe weather event.
  • 运河中估舟、盐船数十艘 - '运河' means 'canal,' '估舟' refers to merchant ships, and '盐船' means salt ships. '数十艘' means 'dozens of ships.'
  • 同时沉没 - Literal phrase meaning 'sunk simultaneously,' indicating that multiple ships sank at the same time.
  • 天方阴晦 - Literal phrase meaning 'the sky was dark and gloomy,' describing the weather before the storm.
  • 众舟泊岸 - Literal phrase meaning 'all the boats docked at the shore,' indicating that the ships were anchored.
  • 各以苇席遮藏 - '苇席' means 'reed mats,' and '遮藏' means 'to cover and hide.' This phrase describes how the ships were covered with reed mats.
  • 舟人均避入舱中 - '舟人' means 'boatmen,' and '避入舱中' means 'took refuge inside the cabins.' This phrase describes how the crew sought safety inside the ships.
  • 一舟席为风揭 - '席' means 'mat,' and '为风揭' means 'was blown away by the wind.' This phrase describes how the reed mat on one ship was blown away by the wind.
  • 榜人出，将覆席 - '榜人' means 'boatman,' and '将覆席' means 'was about to cover the mat.' This phrase describes how a boatman went out to cover the mat.
  • 仰见对面波浪高如山岳 - '仰见' means 'looked up and saw,' '对面' means 'opposite,' and '波浪高如山岳' means 'waves as high as mountains.' This phrase describes the sight of towering waves.
  • 有蓝衣妇人立水上，以衣襟兜物三四枚 - '蓝衣' means 'blue-clothed,' '妇人' means 'woman,' '立水上' means 'standing on the water,' and '以衣襟兜物三四枚' means 'using her sleeves to hold three or four items.' This phrase describes a blue-clothed woman standing on the water, holding items in her sleeves.
  • 状如茄，其色深紫 - '状如茄' means 'shaped like eggplants,' and '其色深紫' means 'its color was deep purple.' This phrase describes the appearance and color of the items.
  • 电光中纤微悉见 - '电光中' means 'in the lightning,' and '纤微悉见' means 'every detail could be seen.' This phrase describes how everything was visible in the lightning.
  • 榜人惊骇倒毙 - '榜人' means 'boatman,' '惊骇' means 'shocked and terrified,' and '倒毙' means 'fell dead.' This phrase describes the boatman's reaction and death.
  • 风狂雨骤 - Literal phrase meaning 'the wind was fierce and the rain was heavy,' describing the intensity of the storm.
  • 遂均遇难 - '遂' means 'thus,' '均' means 'all,' and '遇难' means 'met with disaster.' This phrase describes how all the ships met with disaster.
  • 时邻县有善士，以舟船拯溺尸骸 - '时' means 'at that time,' '邻县' means 'a neighboring county,' '善士' means 'a virtuous person,' '以舟船' means 'using boats,' and '拯溺尸骸' means 'rescuing drowned bodies.' This phrase describes how a virtuous person from a neighboring county used boats to rescue drowned bodies.
  • 见榜人尸顺流而下，救起抚之 - '见' means 'saw,' '榜人尸' means 'the body of the boatman,' '顺流而下' means 'floating downstream,' '救起' means 'rescued,' and '抚之' means 'comforted him.' This phrase describes how the virtuous person saw the boatman's body floating downstream, rescued him, and comforted him.
  • 体尚温，半日而苏 - '体尚温' means 'his body was still warm,' and '半日而苏' means 'he revived after half a day.' This phrase describes how the boatman was still warm and revived after half a day.
  • 其自言所见如此 - '其自言' means 'he said himself,' and '所见如此' means 'what he saw was like this.' This phrase describes how the boatman described what he saw.
  • 盖因一时气闭，水未入腹，是以得活 - '盖因' means 'it is because,' '一时气闭' means 'his breath was temporarily blocked,' '水未入腹' means 'water did not enter his stomach,' and '是以得活' means 'that is why he survived.' This phrase explains why the boatman survived.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 癸酉 - A stem-branch combination in the Chinese calendar system, indicating a specific year.
  • 秋七月夜 - A specific date and time in the Chinese calendar system, indicating the night of the seventh month in autumn.
  • 运河 - Refers to a canal, which was an important mode of transportation in ancient China.
  • 估舟、盐船 - '估舟' refers to merchant ships, and '盐船' means salt ships, indicating the types of ships involved in the event.
  • 榜人 - Refers to a boatman, a common occupation in ancient China.
  • 蓝衣妇人 - Describes a blue-clothed woman, which could be a supernatural or mythological figure in Chinese folklore.
  • 茄 - Refers to eggplants, a common vegetable in Chinese cuisine.
  • 深紫 - Describes a deep purple color, which could be symbolic in Chinese culture.
  • 电光 - Refers to lightning, a natural phenomenon often associated with supernatural events in Chinese folklore.
  • 善士 - Refers to a virtuous person, a common term in Chinese moral and ethical discourse.
  • 拯溺尸骸 - Refers to the act of rescuing drowned bodies, a common theme in Chinese literature and folklore.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 視河中浮尸敗板，飘泊不绝 - Vivid description of a disaster scene: 'Looking at the floating corpses and broken planks in the river, drifting without end.'
  • 不觉大痛 - Literary expression of emotional reaction: 'Unconsciously felt great sorrow.'
  • 计离覆舟处，已隔百余里 - Measured expression of distance: 'Calculating the distance from the place of the capsizing boat, it was already over a hundred li away.'
  • 善士助以资斧，得还里焉 - Classical structure expressing gratitude: 'Kind-hearted people aided with funds and tools, allowing him to return home.'
  • 或云妇人乃鱼精，语不足据 - Colloquial expression of doubt: 'Some say the woman is a fish spirit, but this claim is not reliable.'
  • 第矗立渡上，其为精怪无疑矣 - Concluding statement of certainty: 'Yet she stood upright on the crossing, and it is certain she is a spirit or monster.'
  • 或云是飞天夜叉 - Another speculative statement: 'Some say she is a flying night demon.'
  • 然夜叉偶尔遇之，未有显在云中为众目所共睹者 - Conditional statement: 'Yet night demons are rarely seen, and none have been seen so clearly in the clouds by the eyes of many.'
  • 不知是何怪物 - Final expression of uncertainty: 'It is unknown what kind of monster this is.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 浮尸败板 - Refers to the aftermath of a shipwreck or flood, with corpses and broken wood floating in the river. A common motif in classical Chinese literature to depict disaster or tragedy.
  • 资斧 - Classical term for funds or provisions, often used in the context of travel or assistance. It implies a formal or respectful aid.
  • 鱼精 - Mythical being, a fish spirit or water demon. In Chinese folklore, fish spirits are often associated with rivers and lakes, and can be benevolent or malevolent.
  • 飞天夜叉 - Term from Buddhist or Hindu mythology, referring to a type of supernatural being. In Chinese context, it often denotes a fierce, flying demon or guardian spirit.
  • 夜叉 - General term for a demon or guardian spirit in Buddhist and Hindu traditions. In Chinese literature, it often has a supernatural or monstrous connotation.
  • 云中 - 'In the clouds' is a common setting for supernatural beings or divine figures in classical Chinese texts, suggesting otherworldly presence.
  • 众目所共睹 - Phrase meaning 'seen by many eyes,' indicating public or collective observation. It is used to emphasize the clarity and certainty of a visual event.

Proper Nouns

  • 河南 - Proper noun referring to the province of Henan in China.
  • 黄河 - Proper noun referring to the Yellow River, one of the major rivers in China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 终无确解 - Means 'ultimately without a definitive explanation,' indicating that despite detailed debates, there is no clear resolution.
  • 梦中得句 - Means 'getting poetic lines in a dream,' referring to the phenomenon of receiving inspiration or poetic lines during dreams.
  • 一时灵悟 - Means 'a sudden moment of inspiration,' describing the fleeting and spontaneous nature of such inspiration.
  • 梦中读他人之诗文 - Means 'reading others' poetry and prose in a dream,' indicating a more complex and less common type of dream experience.
  • 扁舟载酒迎波月 - Poetic line meaning 'a small boat carrying wine, welcoming the moonlit waves,' evoking a serene and picturesque scene.
  • 桃花艳滴胭脂血 - Poetic line meaning 'the red of peach blossoms drips like rouge and blood,' creating a vivid and sensual image.
  • 红叶落时征雁返 - Poetic line meaning 'when red leaves fall, the migratory geese return,' symbolizing the cyclical nature of seasons and life.
  • 黄花开后故人来 - Poetic line meaning 'after the chrysanthemums bloom, the old friends return,' suggesting the return of long-lost companions.

  • 载记言梦 - Means 'records of dreams,' referring to historical accounts or writings that mention dreams.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 因删润存之 - Means 'to edit and polish it before keeping it,' indicating the act of revising and preserving the text.
  • 維缑山鹤去之年，庾岭鸿归之月 - A poetic way of referring to a specific time, possibly using allusions to Chinese mythology or poetry. '缑山鹤去' might refer to the legend of the immortal Xiang Yu, and '庾岭鸿归' could be an allusion to the return of geese in autumn.
  • 日逢秋老，时值更阑 - Describes a time of late autumn and late night, with '秋老' meaning 'old autumn' or 'autumn deep,' and '更阑' meaning 'deep into the night.'

The items mentioned are allusions to mythological or poetic imagery.
  • 呜呼！琪花萎秀，竟凋玉女之容；绛草敷荣，莫挽金仙之驾 - A lamentation, where '琪花萎秀' refers to a rare flower losing its beauty, symbolizing the fading of a beautiful woman, and '绛草敷荣' refers to a red grass flourishing, but unable to stop the departure of a divine being.
  • 惟见阶前湘竹，鹃泪斓斑；堪悲窗上茜纱，蛛丝剥落 - Describes the scene of the courtyard and the room, where '湘竹' refers to bamboo from the Xiao-Xiang region, and '鹃泪' refers to the tears of a cuckoo bird, symbolizing sorrow.   • 锦绣丛中过隙，遽成蝶化蚕僵；钗珰队里先鞭，拼得珠沉玉碎 - A metaphor for the fleeting nature of life and beauty, where '锦绣丛中过隙' means passing through a garden of brocade, and '遽成蝶化蚕僵' means quickly turning into a butterfly or becoming stiff like a silkworm. '钗珰队里先鞭' refers to being ahead in a group of beauties, and '拼得珠沉玉碎' means sacrificing precious things.
  • 魂归何处，色即是空；肠断今宵，情殊难已 - A philosophical reflection on the impermanence of life, where '色即是空' is a Buddhist concept meaning that all material forms are ultimately empty. '肠断今宵' means the heart is broken this night, and '情殊难已' means the emotions are hard to bear.

  • 妃子生阀阅之名家，处簪缨之望族 - Describes the noble background of the woman being mourned, where '阀阅' refers to a family with a distinguished history, and '簪缨' refers to high-ranking officials.
  • 孤标冷艳，堪追姑射仙人；弱质温柔，独冠金陵女史 - Describes the woman's unique beauty and grace, where '孤标冷艳' means having a unique and cold beauty, and '姑射仙人' refers to a mythological figure. '弱质温柔' means having a delicate and gentle nature, and '金陵女史' refers to a famous woman from Nanjing.
